title: David Grose

infobox.value: (November 21, 1944 – October 13, 2004) was an American [archaeologist](/wiki/Archaeologist "Archaeologist") and Professor of Classics and Archaeology at the [University of Massachusetts Amherst](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst"). He was an authority on the classification of early ancient [glass](/wiki/Glass "Glass") from the [Roman period](/wiki/Roman_era "Roman era").

["Obituary: David F. Grose, Classics professor"](https://web.archive.org/web/20160305010555/https://www.umass.edu/newsoffice/article/obituary-david-f-grose-classics-professor). University of Massachusetts Amherst. Archived from [the original](https://www.umass.edu/newsoffice/article/obituary-david-f-grose-classics-professor) on March 5, 2016. Retrieved August 11, 2015.
*Classics, Greek, and Latin Majors* (St. Olaf College Classics, Greek, and Latin Majors)[[1]](http://www.stolaf.edu/depts/classics/dept_history/classics_majors.html)
["David Grose research collection"](http://www.cmog.org/library/david-grose-archive). Corning Museum of Glass. Retrieved August 30, 2015.
David F. Grose (1975). [*The Administration of the City of Rome Under the Republic*](https://books.google.com/books?id=JQbbXwAACAAJ). Harvard University.

Department of Art and Art History Oakland University, Rochester MI. Archived from [the original](http://www.csaarome.org/Newsletters/CSAARnews2004.pdf) (PDF) on March 4, 2016. Retrieved August 11, 2015.

*David F. Grose, Professor. Ph. D. Harvard University* (University of Massachusetts Amherst Classics Department)[[2]](http://www.umass.edu/classics/grose.htm) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20100530074439/http://www.umass.edu/classics/grose.htm) 2010-05-30 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American archaeologist

**David F. Grose** (November 21, 1944 – October 13, 2004) was an American [archaeologist](/wiki/Archaeologist "Archaeologist") and Professor of Classics and Archaeology at the [University of Massachusetts Amherst](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst"). He was an authority on the classification of early ancient [glass](/wiki/Glass "Glass") from the [Roman period](/wiki/Roman_era "Roman era").
[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Grose&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

David Frederick Grose was born in the small farming town of [Kenyon, Minnesota](/wiki/Kenyon,_Minnesota "Kenyon, Minnesota"). He was the second child of Frederick and Marie Grose. When he was age 6 years, his family moved to [Austin, Minnesota](/wiki/Austin,_Minnesota "Austin, Minnesota"). He attended Austin Public Schools were his sixth grade teacher, Janet Dickson, remembered that his favorite subject was [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"). He attended [St. Olaf College](/wiki/St._Olaf_College "St. Olaf College") in [Northfield, Minnesota](/wiki/Northfield,_Minnesota "Northfield, Minnesota") were his great uncle, [Ingebrikt Grose](/wiki/Ingebrikt_Grose "Ingebrikt Grose"), had been a professor of Language and Religion shortly after its founding. While at St. Olaf College, Grose went on his first excavation to [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") sites in [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"). He also successfully competed for a [Fulbright Scholarship](/wiki/Fulbright_Program "Fulbright Program") to study [Hadrian's Wall](/wiki/Hadrian%27s_Wall "Hadrian's Wall") in England after he graduated from St. Olaf College in 1966.[[2]](#cite_note-2)
[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Grose&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Education")]

After completing his Fulbright fellowship, Grose attended [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), as a graduate student in the Department of History. He wrote his [doctoral thesis](/wiki/Doctoral_thesis "Doctoral thesis") on the administration of the city of [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") under the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic").[[4]](#cite_note-Grose1975-4) His advisor was Professor Mason Hammond. Grose wrote a considerable portion of his thesis while at the [American Academy in Rome](/wiki/American_Academy_in_Rome "American Academy in Rome") in [Rome, Italy](/wiki/Rome,_Italy "Rome, Italy"). Grose received his [doctoral degree](/wiki/Doctoral_degree "Doctoral degree") from [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard "Harvard") in 1975.
Thereafter, he spent one year at the Museum of Art and Archeology at the [University of Missouri](/wiki/University_of_Missouri "University of Missouri"), in [Columbia, Missouri](/wiki/Columbia,_Missouri "Columbia, Missouri"). While at Missouri, he was strongly influenced by archeologist [Gladys Davidson Weinberg](/wiki/Gladys_Davidson_Weinberg "Gladys Davidson Weinberg") (1909 – 2002) to consider the study of ancient glass. Therefore, he spent another year at the Toledo Museum of Glass in [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio").
[[5]](#cite_note-5)
[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Grose&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Career")]

Subsequently, he accepted a faculty position at the [University of Massachusetts Amherst](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst"). He continued to pursue his interest in Roman glass and spent many summers at the American Academy of Rome. In 1989, he published his authoritative textbook *Early Ancient Glass*. This book was quickly accepted as a definitive description of glass production during ancient Rome. Largely due to this accomplishment, Grose was selected for membership in the prestigious [Society of Antiquaries of London](/wiki/Society_of_Antiquaries_of_London "Society of Antiquaries of London") in 1991.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Later, Grose was chosen to be Chairman of the Department of Classics at the [University of Massachusetts Amherst](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst"). He also was a consultant to the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum") in [London](/wiki/London "London").
[[8]](#cite_note-8)

David Grose died in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts"). His funeral was held in the Chapel of St. Olaf College. He was buried at Gol Lutheran Church cemetery in [Kenyon, Minnesota](/wiki/Kenyon,_Minnesota "Kenyon, Minnesota"). His great grandfather Johan Grose, who had emigrated in 1854 from [Stettin](/wiki/Stettin "Stettin"), [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") and his great grandmother, Ingeborg Ness Grose, who had emigrated in 1854 from [Sogn](/wiki/Sogn "Sogn"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") were founding members of this church.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Grose&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Awards")]

* Fulbright-Hays Fellowship (1966-1967)
* Rakow Award for Excellence in the History of Glass (1984)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## David F. Grose Memorial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Grose&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: David F. Grose Memorial")]

*The David F. Grose Memorial Fund in Classics* was established by the [University of Massachusetts Amherst](/wiki/University_of_Massachusetts_Amherst "University of Massachusetts Amherst"), Department of Classics during the 2003-2004 academic year in order to provide support for our undergraduate students and the programs and scholarships that benefit them.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
